item.title: The Forgotten Birthday: Why John Adams Was Sure America Would Celebrate July 2
item.body:
Here is a piece of trivia with impeccable timing as the nation turns 250: America's independence was actually **voted into existence on July 2, 1776** — and one of the men who made it happen went to his grave believing that was the date we would all celebrate.

## The vote that made a nation

On July 2, 1776, the Second Continental Congress in Philadelphia adopted the **Lee Resolution**, the motion introduced by Virginia's Richard Henry Lee declaring "that these United Colonies are, and of right ought to be, free and independent States." Twelve colonies voted yes; New York abstained, joining later. Legally and politically, that vote *was* independence. What happened two days later, on July 4, was the adoption of the finished text explaining the decision — Thomas Jefferson's Declaration of Independence.

John Adams, independence's fiercest floor advocate, wrote to his wife Abigail the next day in a state of near-euphoria:

> "The Second Day of July 1776, will be the most memorable Epocha, in the History of America... It ought to be solemnized with Pomp and Parade, with Shews, Games, Sports, Guns, Bells, Bonfires and Illuminations from one End of this Continent to the other from this Time forward forever more."

He got every detail right — the parades, the guns, the bonfires, the illuminations — except the date. The Declaration's dateline read July 4, that was the version printed and read aloud across the colonies, and the paperwork beat the vote in the contest for public memory. (Adding to the myth-making: most delegates didn't actually sign the engrossed parchment until August 2.)

## July 2's second act

By poetic coincidence, July 2 earned another place in the American story: on **July 2, 1964**, President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the **Civil Rights Act** into law just hours after the House passed it, with Martin Luther King Jr. standing among the witnesses. The date of the founders' promise became the date of one of its most significant fulfillments — 188 years later, nearly to the hour.

## A thought for the semiquincentennial

As the 250th-anniversary fireworks go up this weekend, spare a toast for the quieter anniversary that just passed. July 4 is when America explained itself to the world. July 2 is when it made up its mind — and as any historian (or any procrastinator) can tell you, making up your mind is the hard part.
